coredata.dc:title: Unveiling the structural and optical properties of N-doped CDs@Y2-xTiWO8: Gd3+ nanocomposites for optical sensing and latent fingerprint detection
coredata.dc:creator: Narwal, Sudha
Nain, Sonia
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as versatile luminescent nanomaterials with excellent optical properties, high stability, and low toxicity, making them ideal candidates for photonic applications. When combined with lanthanide-doped phosphors, these nanocomposites exhibit enhanced luminescence through energy transfer mechanisms, broadening their potential for sensing, imaging, and display technologies. In this study, a series of N-doped CDs@Y2-xTiWO8: Gd3+ (0.02 ≤ x ≤ 0.12) nanocomposites were synthesized, with the 3 mol% Gd-doped compositions displaying strong blue emission (CIE: 0.16, 0.026) and high colour purity (97.88 %), making it a promising candidate for phosphor-based lighting. Structural and morphological analyses using XRD, FE-SEM, and HR-TEM confirmed a single tetragonal phase, with nanophosphor sizes of 0.116 μm and embedded CDs below 10 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) studies revealed that Y3+ efficiently sensitizes Gd3+ emission via dipole-dipole energy transfer under 357 nm near-UV excitation. Notably, the incorporation of N-doped CDs significantly enhanced PL intensity by a factor of 49 through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Lifetime measurements indicated extended decay times from 2.595 μs to 2.808 μs, further supporting the role of CDs in energy mediation. Temperature-dependent PL analysis demonstrated a relative sensitivity of 8 × 10−2 % K−1 at 453 K, underscoring the material's potential for optical thermometry. Additionally, the nanocomposites were successfully employed for latent fingerprint detection, yielding high-contrast images with minimal background interference. These findings establish N-doped CDs@Y2-xTiWO8: Gd3+ as multifunctional materials with promising applications in photonics and forensic science.

head.ce:title: Unveiling the structural and optical properties of N-doped CDs@Y2-xTiWO8: Gd3+ nanocomposites for optical sensing and latent fingerprint detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Deenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology, Murthal 131039, Haryana, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Deenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Deenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology, Murthal, 131039, Haryana, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as versatile luminescent nanomaterials with excellent optical properties, high stability, and low toxicity, making them ideal candidates for photonic applications. When combined with lanthanide-doped phosphors, these nanocomposites exhibit enhanced luminescence through energy transfer mechanisms, broadening their potential for sensing, imaging, and display technologies. In this study, a series of N-doped CDs@Y2-xTiWO8: Gd3+ (0.02 ≤ x ≤ 0.12) nanocomposites were synthesized, with the 3 mol% Gd-doped compositions displaying strong blue emission (CIE: 0.16, 0.026) and high colour purity (97.88 %), making it a promising candidate for phosphor-based lighting. Structural and morphological analyses using XRD, FE-SEM, and HR-TEM confirmed a single tetragonal phase, with nanophosphor sizes of 0.116 μm and embedded CDs below 10 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) studies revealed that Y3+ efficiently sensitizes Gd3+ emission via dipole-dipole energy transfer under 357 nm near-UV excitation. Notably, the incorporation of N-doped CDs significantly enhanced PL intensity by a factor of 49 through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Lifetime measurements indicated extended decay times from 2.595 μs to 2.808 μs, further supporting the role of CDs in energy mediation. Temperature-dependent PL analysis demonstrated a relative sensitivity of 8 × 10−2 % K−1 at 453 K, underscoring the material's potential for optical thermometry. Additionally, the nanocomposites were successfully employed for latent fingerprint detection, yielding high-contrast images with minimal background interference. These findings establish N-doped CDs@Y2-xTiWO8: Gd3+ as multifunctional materials with promising applications in photonics and forensic science.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings can be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Inorganic compounds doped with rare earth (RE) have attracted much interest because of their remarkable qualities and broad uses. These compounds are outstanding materials for lighting and display technologies because of their high quantum efficiency, precise emission spectra, and crisp emission lines [1–6]. RE-doped inorganic materials' unique optical characteristics guarantee excellent and energy-efficient lighting [7–16]. With their customised light spectra, these materials are essential to agriculture's optimisation of plant development conditions, as they improve photosynthesis. The remarkable thermal and electrical characteristics of RE-doped phosphors, in addition to their eco-friendliness, increase their usefulness in a range of industrial settings. The main focus of current research is on improving the performance of these materials by the exploration of novel compositions and doping strategies that can improve their characteristics.

Selecting the appropriate host matrix and activator ion is critical for producing profitable luminescent compounds. Borate, titanate, silicate, sulphate and tungstate are examples of prominent host lattices [4,8,17–34]. Trivalent rare earth elements (REEs) are considered excellent activators for optical materials, serving as structural probes. Because of its high average refraction coefficient and great chemical stability, tungstate is an exclusive metal oxide that effectively transfers energy from the host matrix to the targeted levels of the doped ions. This improves colour purity and fluorescence brilliance. The utility of tungstate is increased by its great efficacy in transmitting energy to RE3+ ion emission sites and absorbing near-ultraviolet radiation [1,35–40].

Phosphor materials typically emit light due to the f-f or f-d transitions associated with RE ions. Adding dopant metal ions significantly alters the physical-chemical properties of host matrices, especially at the microscopic level. This allows for tuning defect ions by adjusting their coupling behaviour with the host lattice. Rare-earth ions produce sharp emission spectra with high colour purity due to intra-transitions in the 4f-4f orbitals, making them ideal for luminous screens, microelectronics and optronic gadgets. Gd3+ ions, known for their biomedical applications and non-toxic nature, are also being explored for optoelectronic devices.

Given a smaller coordination degree of atoms on the surface, quantum dots (QDs), with their high surface-to-volume ratio, induce defect states on the surface of nanoparticles. Through surface modification and sensitization, QDs can adjust the optical band gap of target materials to boost photoluminescence (PL). Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are popular for their lower toxicity, high reliability, and bright luminescence [41–50]. Surface states have a major impact on luminous peak intensity and quantum efficiency in QDs.

Luminescence thermometry offers non-contact thermal sensing with high sensitivity and resolution, addressing challenges associated with conventional methods [51–54]. Rare-earth metal doped oxides are popular for thermometry probes due to their reduced toxic exposure, desirable chemical, thermal, and photo-stability, and large bandwidths [55–58]. Fingerprints contain a range of data, from friction ridge patterns to the metabolic rates of the individuals. Consequently, fingerprint detection has garnered significant interest in areas such as criminal investigation, medical diagnosis, and health evaluation. The most common type of fingerprints encountered are latent fingerprints (LFPs), which are particularly challenging because they are present but not visible. One of the earliest and most widely used methods for visualizing LFPs at crime scenes is powder dusting, known for its simplicity, effectiveness, and ease of use. Extreme brightness and stability in abrasive chemical and heat environments are two qualities that make phosphors activated by rare-earth metal ions an excellent choice for materials used in LFP visualisation, whether in powder or wet form [59–64].

This report presents the synthesis and investigation of CDs impregnated on gadolinium-doped Y2TiWO8 blue-emitting nanocomposite with good temperature-sensing properties. Various characterizations, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), and high-resolution tunnelling electron microscopy (HR-TEM), were performed to study the developed phosphors. The optical properties, concentration quenching, decay time, colour purity, CIE coordinates, and correlated colour temperature (CCT) values of the synthesized nanocomposite were also examined. The results demonstrate the potential applications of the CDs@Y2TiWO8: Gd3+ nanocomposite for blue-emitting LED B1 lighting, optical temperature sensors, and latent fingerprint detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Y2TiWO8 and Y2-xTiWO8: xGd3+(0.02 ≤ x ≤ 0.12) nano phosphors
ce:section.ce:para: The Y2TiWO8 and Y2-xTiWO8: xGd3+(0.02 ≤ x ≤ 0.12) nano phosphors were synthesized using the sol-gel method. The primary materials used included Y(NO3)3.5H2O, Gd(NO3)3.6H2O, Ti(OC4H9)4, and Na2WO4.2H2O with 99.9 % purity. Firstly, prepare a solution of yttrium nitrate, gadolinium nitrate and Na2WO4.2H2O in deionised water and a solution of Ti(OC4H9)4 in ethanol (40 ml) and deionised water (10 ml) mixture. Secondly, mixed the nitrate solution into the solution of Ti(OC4H9)4 and then gradually added the solution of Na2WO4.2H2O which immediately resulted in a white sol. The resulting solution was vigorously stirred for 2 h at 75 °C. Then, the gel was air-dried at 80 °C for 12 h. Thirdly, the sample was pre-sintered at 500 °C for 5 h. Lastly, the sample was annealed at 900 °C for 4 h which produced the desired nano phosphor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped CDs@Y1.94TiWO8: (0.06) Gd3+ nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Urea and Citric acid were taken as precursors for the Carbon Dots. Urea: Citric Acid (in the ratio 1:2) was mixed with 10 ml of distilled water. Ten minutes were spent stirring the mixture. Then 5 ml water along with 0.05 g nano phosphor was dissolved together. After agitating the solution for ten more minutes, it was completely mixed and put into a 100 ml autoclave, where it was left to react for 5 h at 200 °C. The solution was cooled and then rinsed with ethanol before being centrifuged thrice at 4300 rpm to yield the sample. Dialysis was unnecessary because the N-doped CDs have been embedded on the Y1.94TiWO8: (0.06) Gd3+ (YTiWO: Gd3+) substrate. The sample was air-dried at 80 °C for 6 h before further analysis and application.

Using a third-generation Empyrean XRD from Malvern Panalytical, featuring Cu K-alpha radiation (λ = 0.154 nm), the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the synthesized nanophosphors were analyzed. The recorded XRD data covered a 2θ range from 10° to 70°. To characterize the morphology and elemental composition, high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) were employed, using a 7610F Plus/JEOL system. Additionally, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was performed with a TECNAI 200 Kv TEM (Fei, Electron Optics). Photoluminescence spectra and lifetime decay measurements were obtained using a QuantaMaster 8450–22 Spectrofluorometer (Horiba), equipped with a 450 W xenon lamp as the excitation source.

XRD data is used to determine the structure, phase, composition, shape, size and crystallinity of the sample. Fig. 1 (a) shows the XRD patterns for Y2TiWO8 and Y2-xTiWO8: xGd3+ (0.0 ≤ x ≤ 0.12). The Y2TiWO8 and Y2-xTiWO8: xGd3+ were synthesized by sol-gel method at a temperature of 900 °C. Their diffraction patterns are in agreement with JCPDS card no. 22-0998 confirming single phase, i.e., tetragonal phase with space group I 41/a. Fig. 1 (b) shows the crystal structure of Y2TiWO8. Moreover, the peaks are sharp and fine which implies that the samples are pure as well as crystalline in nature. We observed that as the dopant ion concentration increases, the intense peak at 2Ɵ = 28.97°, having d-spacing value = 3.084 Å and plane (112), shifts towards a lower angle, suggesting the addition of Gadolinium (III) ion into the matrix. It was further noted that when the concentration of dopant ion Gd3+ increased to 6 mol%, there was a change in the diffraction pattern. The peak (marked with *) could be due to the expansion caused by excess of Gd3+ dopant ion concentration resulting in the variation of the phase and crystal structure. The YTiWO: Gd3+ phosphor was further coated with Carbon Dots (CDs) by hydrothermal treatment to obtain the final CDs@YTiWO: Gd3+ composite, Fig. 1(c) [65]. The XRD data of synthesized CDs in Fig. 1(d) shows a hump at a 2θ value of 20° which can be ascribed to the (002) plane of amorphous carbon. The absence of a distinct signature for CDs in the XRD pattern of CDs@ Y1.94TiWO8: (0.06)Gd3+ can be attributed to the amorphous nature of CDs. Carbon dots (CDs) typically do not exhibit sharp diffraction peaks but instead contribute to a broad hump around 20° (2θ) due to their graphitic or disordered carbon structure. While this feature is observed in Fig. 1(c), it is not prominently visible in Fig. 1(b), likely because the strong diffraction peaks of the host matrix overshadow it [66]. Additionally, as reported by [67], the absence of CDs' characteristic signature in the XRD pattern may also be due to their low content in the composite material, falling below the detection limit of the diffractometer. As a result, the XRD patterns do not exhibit distinct diffraction peaks associated with either crystalline or amorphous carbon. Further, it was observed that the peak intensities were stronger with the introduction of CDs, and the FWHM (full width at half-maxima) reduced to 50 %. Due to the introduction of CDs into lattice, some redox reactions are taking place which increase the crystallinity of the host matrix. [45]

The successful replacement of Y3+ ion by dopant Gd3+ ion can also be confirmed theoretically by calculating the radius difference percentage (Dr). The difference percentage between the radii of replaced and doping ion should have a smaller value (Dr < 30 %) for achieving the desired replacement. As we know, the ionic radii of Y3+ ions and Gd3+ ions are 1.019 and 1.053 Å, respectively, hence, Dr can be calculated by using the following equation [68]:
(1)
D
r =
Y − G
/ G × 100 %
where Y is the ionic radii of the replaced ion (Y3+) and G is the ionic radii of the dopant ion (Gd3+). Using the above equation, it was concluded that the Dr = 3.22 %, which is <30 %. Hence, Gd3+ ions have succeeded in substituting the Y3+ ions resulting in the formation of the desired phosphor material.

The host lattice's crystal structure is altered by the introduction of dopant ions. These changes are observed by the broadening of the XRD diffraction lines. The XRD diffraction lines are broadened primarily due to the combination of the size of the crystallite and structural micro-strain. The crystallite size of the synthesized phosphors can be calculated with the help of the Debye-Scherrer formula: Ð = (0.9 x λ) / (β x cosƟ), where Ð is the average crystallite size, λ is X-rays wavelength, β is the FWHM and Ɵ is the angle [69]. Using the equation, the mean size of the crystallite was 7.44 nm. Further, the lattice strain was calculated using the following formula: ε = F.W.H.M. / (4 × tanƟ) and the value of the lattice microstrain was found to be 0.0194 [70].

To obtain insights into the microstructure, morphology and particle size of the synthesized nanocomposite, FE-SEM analysis was performed. The samples were gold sputtered before performing the analysis to improve image clarity. Fig. 2(a) displays the FE-SEM image of the synthesized CDs@Y1.94TiWO8:(0.06)Gd3+ nanocomposite at a magnification of x25,000 and a scale of 1 μm. The image reveals that the particles exhibit irregular cubic morphologies, with variations in particle size. The absence of significant agglomeration indicates a well-dispersed particle distribution, which is essential for achieving uniform structural and functional properties in the composite. To provide a more detailed observation, Fig. 2(b) presents a higher-magnification (x75,000) FE-SEM image at a scale of 100 nm. This close-up view enables the identification of Carbon Dots (CDs) deposited on the surface of the Y1.94TiWO8:(0.06)Gd3+ matrix. The deposition of Carbon Dots enhances the material's optical and electronic properties, making it suitable for advanced functional applications such as luminescence and photocatalysis.

The particle size distribution of the synthesized Y1.94TiWO8:(0.06)Gd3+ nanocomposite was evaluated using statistical analysis of the SEM images. The histogram in Fig. 2(c) represents the particle size distribution, with an average particle diameter calculated to be approximately 0.161 μm. The distribution curve indicates a relatively narrow size range, reflecting the uniformity of the synthesis process. This size homogeneity is critical for consistent performance in potential applications. In contrast, the size distribution of the Carbon Dots is presented in Fig. 2(d). The histogram shows that the particle size of the CDs is predominantly below 10 nm, with the majority of particles concentrated in the range of 3–10 nm. The narrow size distribution and nanoscale dimensions of the CDs are indicative of their high surface-to-volume ratio, which plays a pivotal role in enhancing their functional properties, including optical absorption and surface reactivity.

To further validate the purity, elemental composition, and homogeneity of the synthesized samples, energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX/EDAX) and elemental mapping were conducted. Fig. 3(a) presents the EDX spectrum of the CDs@ Y1.94TiWO8: (0.06)Gd3+ nanocomposite, revealing characteristic peaks corresponding to the elements Y, Ti, W, O, Gd, C, and N. The presence of these peaks confirms the incorporation of the intended elements in the synthesized sample without any detectable impurities. The appearance of a gold (Au) peak is attributed to the gold sputtering performed during sample preparation to enhance conductivity for imaging and analysis. Fig. 3(b) illustrates the elemental mapping of the nanocomposite, showing the spatial distribution of each element across the sample. The mapping indicates that all elements (Y, Ti, W, O, Gd, C, and N) are homogeneously dispersed and evenly distributed throughout the nanocomposite matrix. This uniform distribution is critical for ensuring consistent material properties and optimal performance in potential applications. The combination of EDX spectra and elemental mapping confirms the successful synthesis of pure and compositionally homogeneous nanostructured material. Together with the FE-SEM analysis, these results highlight the material's suitability for advanced applications in optoelectronics, luminescence, and related fields.

To gain deeper insight into the dimensional and structural characteristics of the synthesized nanoparticles, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) analysis was performed. The HR-TEM image in Fig. 4 reveals that the nanoparticles exhibit irregular shapes and sizes, consistent with observations from SEM analysis. The presence of Carbon Dots is visible, as highlighted by the yellow circles. These Carbon Dots display a roughly spherical morphology and appear to be uniformly distributed on the surface of the nanoparticles. This even dispersion underscores the effectiveness of the synthesis process in achieving a homogeneous composite structure. The inset in Fig. 4 shows a high-magnification view of the lattice fringes. A measured interplanar spacing (d-spacing) of 0.25 nm is observed, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [71,72]. This specific d-spacing aligns well with the graphitic structure of Carbon Dots and confirms the presence of a sp2 hybridized carbon network. The well-defined lattice fringes further validate the high degree of structural order in the Carbon Dots, which is crucial for their functional properties. In conclusion, the HR-TEM analysis provides critical evidence of the successful synthesis of nanoparticles with uniformly distributed Carbon Dots and confirms their graphitic crystalline nature, supporting their potential application in advanced functional materials.

The PL excitation plot of Gd3+- doped Y2TiWO8 are exhibited in Fig. 5(a). The excitation data ranging from 300 to 420 nm consists of five peaks at 339 nm, 357 nm (highest intensity), 368 nm, 376 nm and 395 nm. The broadband blue emission ranging from 380 to 580 nm with a tail at a longer wavelength was observed with a peak centred at 425 nm. Moreover, with the addition of the Gd3+ ion, it was observed that as the number of emission centres increased, the emission intensity (λems = 422 nm) was also increased when excited at λ = 357 nm, Fig. 5(b). The increment in emission intensity was observed up to 3 mol% concentration and on further increasing the Gd3+ ion concentration, there was a decline in the emission intensity that is due to concentration quenching (detailed in section 3.4). Generally, it is noted that the Gd3+ ions emit at λ = 310–320 nm but here an exceptional behaviour of Gd3+ ions emission at 422 nm (possibly arising from the 6P7/2 or higher states to the less energetic levels 8S7/2) is noted. In oxide systems, point, and stoichiometric defects as well as vacancies of oxygen are often very common, and their presence greatly affects the altered PL behaviour [73,74]. According to reports, multi-ionization of Gadolinium (III) ions provides numerous levels of impurity, leading to distinct crossovers and bluish radiation. The effective insertion of Gd3+ ions into the host matrix is confirmed by the augmented blue luminescence.

To further improve the PL emission magnitude of the as-synthesized nano phosphors, CDs doped with nitrogen were coated on the surface of YTiWO: Gd3+ nano phosphors via the hydrothermal method. Carcassing and surface modification under reduced temperatures have been proven to produce effective CDs. Citric Acid was chosen as the initial source of carbon because of its relatively low carbonization temperature. Doping with nitrogen is further shown to alter the count of electrons, which contributes to the host material's increased photoluminescence. Fig. 5(c) shows the PL emission intensity spectra of CDs@ YTiWO: Gd3+. As we can see, when excited at 357 nm, the PL emission intensity of CDs@YTiWO: Gd3+ increased by a factor of 49 as compared to YTiWO: Gd3+ along with the broadening of FWHM, having a peak centred at 432 nm. Such red-shift in the emission and rise in the photoluminescence intensity of the nanocomposite can be attributed to the presence of N-doped Carbon Dots. The N-doped CDs have size-dependent electronic states, so, their bandgap decreases as their size increases. The incorporation of CDs introduces new energy levels within the host material, leading to a lower energy transition, which results in a red shift in the emission spectrum.

In pure YTiWO: Gd3+, the Y3+ ions are excited by the UV light and then energy is transferred from the Y3+ ions to the various excited levels of Gd3+ ion which decays radiatively to lower energy levels of Gd3+ ions. But when the CDs are also incorporated into the matrix, the CDs are excited to their excited states along with Y3+ ions and transfer the energy to excited levels of Gd3+ ions.

The proposed explanation for the energy transfer among Y3+, Gd3+ and CDs is the FRET mechanism [75,76]. FRET is a non-radiative energy transfer that occurs through dipole-dipole interactions over distances typically around 1–10 nm. A key criterion for FRET is spectral overlap. The emission spectrum of the donor (Carbon Dots) must overlap with the absorption or excitation spectrum of the acceptor as shown in Fig. 6 (a). Because of the cross-section of the absorption and emission bands of Y3+ ions (250–350 nm) and the CDs (300-600 nm), the CDs transfer their excited electrons to the more energetic states of the Y (III) ion. Secondly, FRET is most effective when the donor and acceptor are 1–10 nm apart. HR-TEM image {under section 3.2.2; Fig. 4} shows CDs embedded in the nanophosphor matrix at <10 nm, supporting the possibility of FRET.

A Sequential Transfer (Carbon Dots → Y3+ → Gd3+) is more likely due to the complex energy levels in the YTiWO matrix, which can act as a mediator. Ti and W have variable oxidation states (Ti4+, W6+), which can support charge transfer transitions within the host matrix, enhancing the energy transfer efficiency by providing pathways for excited electrons. Furthermore, Ti and W can create localized energy states within the matrix due to their d-orbital configurations, potentially creating intermediate energy levels within the host's band gap. These levels can act as stepping stones for energy transfer within the matrix, facilitating energy migration between CDs, Y3+, and Gd3+ ions. Energy is transferred non-radiatively from CDs to nearby Ti or W sites within the YTiWO matrix. Ti and W may serve as relay stations, absorbing and redistributing energy to other parts of the matrix. The energy moves through the lattice until it reaches Y3+ ions. In the final energy transfer step, the number of electrons increases in the excited energy states of Y3+ ions which can further proficiently channel energy to the 6P7/2 or higher states of Gd3+ ions, and radiantly degrade to less energetic levels (8S7/2) of Gd3+ ion, enhancing the blue photoluminescence. This sequential transfer provides an efficient pathway as the YTiWO matrix absorbs energy from the CDs and relays it to Gd3+ ions.

In summary, the energy transfer mechanism involves the excitation of Carbon Dots, non-radiative sequential transfer of energy to Gd3+ ions via the FRET mechanism, and subsequent emission from the Gd3+ ions at 432 nm. When compared to YTiWO: Gd3+, CDs@YTiWO: Gd3+ acquire higher levels of energy with fewer non-radiative emissions, which enhances blue emission. Fig. 6 shows the comparative PL emission intensity spectra of Y1.94TiWO8: (0.06) Gd3+ and CDs@Y1.94TiWO8:(0.06) Gd3+.

Concentration quenching occurs when the concentration of the activator ion increases causing a reduction in the fluorescence intensity. Such a situation arises when two dopant ions interact with one another and result in non-radiative energy exchange. To examine how the Gd3+ ion concentration affects the synthesized nanophosphors' PL intensity, the concentration of Gd3+ ion varied from 0 mol% to 6 mol%. It was observed that as the concentration of Gd3+ ion was increased up to 3 mol%, there was a boost in the PL emission intensity. On further increasing the Gd3+ ion concentration, a decrease in the emission intensity was observed which can be due to the concentration quenching. The proximity between nearby Gd3+ ions gets closer as the number of dopant ions rises, bringing about a reduction in the degree of fluorescence and amplification of the transferred energy employing a non-radiative occurrence. Thus, the concentration x = 3 mol% is thought to be the optimal concentration for dopant ion Gd3+.

Three main mechanisms can generally be found governing energy transmission among neighbouring Gd3+ ions: the exchange interaction, radiation reabsorption, and electric multipolar contact phenomena. Since there is no discernible excitation-emission spectrum overlap, the potential of radiation reabsorption may be ruled out. Moreover, the critical distance, Rc, for efficient transfer of energy needs to be taken into account to narrow down the real mechanism between exchange contact and electric multipolar interactions. As a result, Rc is computed using the following equation proposed by Blasse [77]:
(2)
R c
= 2
3 V
4 π
X c
N
1 / 3
N denotes replaceable ions. Xc is the preeminent conc., V is volume and the value of Rc = 16.670 Å.

According to earlier research, the value of Rc between nearby activator ions should be <5 Å for the transfer of energy. Consequently, electric multipolar interactions— that could be divided into three types: dipole-dipole (d-d), dipole-quadrupole (d-q), and quadrupole-quadrupole (q-q) interactions—remain the sole viable means of transferring energy for larger Rc value. To know the right type of multipolar interactions, we used Dexter's equation for the transfer of energy [78]:
(3)
I x
= K
1 + β
x
Q 3
− 1
where Q is the multipolar interactions constant and equals 6, 8, and 10 for different interactions. K and β are invariables of known hosts for identical stimulation situations. I/x is the Emission strength per number of dopant ions. Plotting the log (I/x) against log (x) results linearly having a slope value equal to −2.08356. This means that the Q value is equal to 6.4, which is not too far from the theoretical value of 6, Fig. 7 . These findings show that the energy transfer and concentration quenching effect in the YTiWO: Gd3+ nano phosphors are mainly caused by dipole-dipole interactions.

Lifetime decay is pivotal to both fundamental science and practical applications. Studying the decay time can help differentiate between light-emitting and energy-dissipating processes, study the energy transfer mechanisms, and study the effect of varying concentration to determine the optimal concentration of the dopant ion for maximum performance for various display technologies. Fig. 8(a), (b) and (c) photoluminescence decay profiles of Y1.94TiWO8: (0.06) Gd3+, CDs@ Y1.94TiWO8: (0.06) Gd3+ and CDs respectively. The decay curves are well-fitted by the two exponentials [75]:

In the exponential composition, τ1 and τ2 represent the fluorescence decay time, and A1 and A2 are constant parameters. The following formula can be used to get the average fluorescence decay time based on such factors [75]:

A prolonged decay time often indicates the presence of energy migration and re-emission processes, the calculated mean decay time for the YTiWO: Gd3+ and CDs@ YTiWO: Gd3+ is 2.595 μs and 2.808 μs, respectively. The observed increase in the average decay time for CDs@YTiWO: Gd3+ confirms an energy transfer mechanism from N-doped Carbon Dots (CDs) to Gd3+ ions via Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). In this process, the CDs act as donors, transferring energy non-radiatively to the Gd3+ ions (acceptors), thereby prolonging the decay time due to delayed emission from the Gd3+ ions. A key characteristic of FRET is a decrease in donor lifetime (=3.33 μs) when an acceptor is introduced. We have compared the photoluminescence decay profiles of Y1.94TiWO8: (0.06) Gd3+, CDs@ Y1.94TiWO8: (0.06) Gd3+ and CDs {Fig. 8 (a), (b) and (c), respectively} to demonstrate lifetime quenching, a strong indicator of non-radiative energy transfer.

Accurate CIE coordinate determination is crucial for uses such as white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs), where a harmonious blend of red, green, and blue emissions is required to produce superior-grade whitish LEDs. The hue depiction, effectiveness, and general functionality of the luminaire and visual systems can all be improved by nano phosphors that have customised CIE coordinates. Nano phosphors are evaluated by converting their emission spectra into CIE coordinates. The CIE standard observer functions, which characterize the average colour perception of human vision, must be integrated with the spectral power distribution to achieve this. On the CIE chromaticity diagram, the resulting x and y coordinates identify the precise colour that the nano phosphor is emitting. The CIE coordinates diagram for the synthesized YTiWO: Gd3+ nano phosphor and CDs@ YTiWO: Gd3+ nanocomposite is displayed in Fig. 9(a) and (b). The YTiWO: Gd3+ and CDs@ YTiWO: Gd3+ samples' CIE coordinates are all close to the boundary of the blue region. Furthermore, colour purity is another important factor to consider when assessing the performance of nano phosphors in applications such as lighting and display technology. It measures how vibrant and saturated the colour is, or how near it is to a single wavelength, often known as monochromatic light. The mathematical formula for colour purity is [79]:
(6)

The colour purity is given in Table 1 for the YTiWO: Gd3+ and CDs@ YTiWO: Gd3+ samples. The colour purity of the CDs@ YTiWO: Gd3+ sample was slightly reduced as compared to the YTiWO: Gd3+ which is due to the variation in the CIE coordinates of the samples. This variation arises due to the red-shift in the emission wavelength of the CDs@ YTiWO: Gd3+ samples.

McCamy proposed a cubic approximation to calculate the Correlated Colour Temperature values. [10]

The CIE coordinates of the nanocomposite are shown here by (x, y). The epicentre chromaticity coordinates are (xe, ye) = (0.332, 0.186). The resultant CDs@YTiWO: Gd3+ nano phosphor exhibits a CCT of 1757.03 K. Warm light-look CCTs are defined as those with a temperature below 4000 K. Consequently, the resulting nano phosphor has potential uses, including Ultraviolet-Light Emitting Diodes [68].

Fig. 10 displays the PL spectra of CDs@ YTiWO: Gd3+ that were examined between 283 and 453 K in temperature. The study of temperature-dependent photoluminescence (PL) examines how a material's emission characteristics vary with temperature. The thermal energy produced by rising temperatures can make non-radiative relaxation processes more pronounced, which lowers PL intensity. This behaviour contributes to our understanding of photoluminescent materials' efficiency and thermal stability. Peak positions may move in some materials as a result of phase transitions or thermal expansion, but in other materials, the peak positions stay constant, suggesting strong performance in a range of temperature environments. Temperature variations reduced the intensity of the nano phosphor but did not change the peak's position. Consequently, the higher thermal stability of CDs@ YTiWO: Gd3+ nano phosphor may be demonstrated. For applications such as LEDs, where maintaining constant emission characteristics at varying operating temperatures is critical, temperature-dependent PL analysis is required. Fig. 10 shows that the emission intensity at 383 K was 84 % of that at 283 K. Consequently, a promising LED phosphor might be the CDs@ YTiWO: Gd3+ nano phosphor, which has exceptional thermal stability.

As temperature rises, the average lifespan of the excited state and emission intensity decrease due to the thermal quenching effect. At higher temperatures, it also broadens the spectral bandwidth because thermally excited electrons can reach higher vibrational states. The configurational coordinate diagram, which takes nonradiative transitions through the ground-state–excited-state crossover point into account, can be used to explain this occurrence. The activation energy of thermal quenching is the energy difference between this crossover point and the lowest state of the excited state. The following Arrhenius equation [8] can be used to calculate this activation energy by taking into account both emission intensity (I) and Temperature (T) [80].
(8)

The plot of ln[{Ϊ0/ Ϊ}-1] and 1/(ǩͲ) has been illustrated in Fig. 11 (a). Following linear fit, Εα was successfully estimated to be 0.20 eV. Taking the Sr value into account is crucial for ensuring reliable findings when utilising optical thermometry. Sr can be computed using the following formula: [80].

Eq. (9) was used for calculating the Sr results, displayed in Fig. 11(b) as a temperature measurement. The calculated Sr findings rise along with temperature, reaching the highest Sr magnitude of 8 × 10−2 % K −1 at 453 K. These outcomes are comparable to those obtained from various fluorescent detectors (Table 2 ).

Another branch of science that has been heavily utilised to help law enforcement identify criminals and solve crimes is forensic technology. To provide better coating, greater contrast, higher resolution fingerprint images, and better ridge features in daylight, various metal oxide powders are applied to surfaces left behind by fingerprints. These characteristics support the validity of latent fingerprint crime scene identification. Additionally, quantum dots have demonstrated increased fluorescence; as a result, their composites with metal oxide nanoparticles have the potential to be highly effective in fingerprint detection due to improvements in image quality, visibility, ridge characteristics, background contrast, selectivity, and sensitivity.

The powder approach is the most widely used technique for latent fingerprint detection. To collect fingerprints, this method applies powdered materials to the area surrounding the crime scene, where they are adsorbed on perspiration residue, secretions, and eccrine (98 % water and 20 % moisture) on the fingers.

Here, we have detected the latent fingerprints using the CDs@YTiWO: Gd3+ nanocomposite. Fig. 12 (a) shows the fingerprints under normal light and (b) under UV light. The fingerprints exhibit blue luminescence when exposed to UV light, which brightens and improves contrast and visibility in photographs. The fingerprints are further categorised in Fig. 13 according to various levels of fingerprint recognition. Level 1 consists of the core, loop, whorls, arcs, etc.; Level 2 consists of ridges, bifurcations, lakes, deltas, etc.; Level 3 consists of sweat pores. The procedure of identification entails comparing the discovered fingerprint from the crime scene with the control fingerprint that is kept on file, as well as matching the specifics of the ridge pattern. Generally, the sensitivity and resolution of the fingerprints reduce over time due to loss of water and chemical components, so, to check the longevity and durability of the phosphor, we aged the samples for 20 days and examined the fingerprints under 365 nm UV light Fig. 12(c). It can be seen that the phosphor powder was nicely adhered to the fingermarks and the ridge characteristics were well contrasted with the background. Hence, the CDs@YTiWO: Gd3+ nanocomposite has proven to be durable and a stable material for latent fingerprint detection.

Due to their advantageous optical and chemical characteristics, fluorescent QD powders and rare earth fluorescent nanomaterials have become new tools for the development of latent fingerprints. Their advantages include small size, superior chemical characteristics, photostability, high fluorescence, good surface modification, and minimal toxicity. Therefore, CDs@YTiWO: Gd3+ nanocomposite can be an excellent tool for latent fingerprint detection.

In conclusion, the versatile possibilities offered by YTiWO and CDs@YTiWO: Gd3+ systems in temperature monitoring and identification of latent fingerprints are the focus of this work. To study the crystal structure and morphology, XRD, FE-SEM and HR-TEM were conducted and it was concluded that the nanophosphors had a single tetragonal phase and the average particle size of 0.116 μm of the nanophosphors and <10 nm of the embedded Carbon Dots. The successful replacement of Y3+ ion by dopant Gd3+ ion was confirmed theoretically by calculating the radius difference percentage (Dr = 3.22 %). A thorough examination of the synthesized nanocomposite's PL characteristics is conducted. Gd3+ exhibits extraordinary blue luminescence under UV light excitation at 357 nm. The PL magnitude was amplified 49 times after the impregnation of the CDs on the YTiWO: Gd3+ surface. It is discovered that 0.06 mol of Gd3+ is the ideal concentration for doping. The decay time of the synthesized Y1.94TiWO8: (0.06) Gd3+ and CDs@ Y1.94TiWO8: (0.06) Gd3+ were calculated to be 2.595 μs and 2.808 μs, respectively. Additionally, with a very high colour purity of 97.88 %, the nanocomposite's CIE coordinates are (0.16, 0.026). To gain further insight into thermal quenching, the temperature-dependent PL is also examined. Compared to its initial intensity, the nanocomposite exhibits 84 % intensity at 383 K. To determine the nanocomposite's potential in optical thermometry, its relative sensitivity was further computed to be 8 × 10−2 % K −1 at 453 K. Additionally, the powder brushing technique is used to examine the nanocomposite's potential for latent fingerprint recognition. The fluorescent pictures of LFPs are well contrasted against the dark backdrop. It is easy to see the contoured formations under 365 nm UV light, indicating that particles are exclusively found on the raised areas. In addition to the basic level, the current phosphors make it simple to collect second and third-level details, such as sweat pores, deltas, and bifurcations. In summary, the current study demonstrates the intriguing possibilities of the CDs@YTiWO: Gd3+ nanocomposite in a variety of applications, which spurs additional exploration in this domain.
